skill.name: runcomfy-cli
skill.description: Use the RunComfy command-line tool to authenticate, run hosted AI media models, poll jobs, and download generated outputs.
skill.summary: Run RunComfy AI image, video, edit, and training models from the command line.
skill.author: doany-ai

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, and filesystem patterns, but the reviewed file is a Markdown skill guide for the RunComfy CLI rather than executable source code. The external command and network behavior is intentional for this CLI skill, while the weak-crypto and path-traversal alerts appear to be false positives from prose and example output. Publish with a warning because the skill can cause authenticated remote model execution, file downloads, and local token handling.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated Remote Model Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill is designed to run RunComfy model jobs through the CLI. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it sends user input to a remote API and may incur account usage or billing.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines explicitly document submitting JSON bodies to RunComfy and downloading remote outputs. The behavior is intentional, but it is security-relevant because it uses authenticated network execution.

medium_findings.title: Local Token Storage and Secret Handling
medium_findings.description: The authentication flow stores an API token in a local config file or accepts RUNCOMFY_TOKEN from the environment. This is expected for a CLI, but token exposure would give access to the user account.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token storage path and environment variable are explicitly documented. The skill also warns not to log or commit the token, which reduces but does not remove the risk.

medium_findings.title: Remote Output Downloads to Local Paths
medium_findings.description: The CLI downloads generated files to the current directory or a user-selected output directory. This can write large remote outputs to disk and should be used with controlled paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file-write behavior is clear from the command options and download description. A documented 2 GiB cap reduces disk-fill risk but does not eliminate local file-write concerns.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Trigger Static Command Alerts
low_findings.description: Many external command alerts are from fenced examples such as install commands, status polling, shell loops, and jq usage. They are documentation examples, not hidden executable code inside the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible Markdown examples and the skill declares a narrower Bash(runcomfy *) tool scope. Some examples still require user care if copied into a shell.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts at the cited lines do not correspond to cryptographic code. The cited content is metadata description and an exit-code section heading.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no hashing or encryption operation at these locations. The static scanner appears to have matched incidental text, not executable crypto usage.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Alert Is Example URL Text
low_findings.description: The path traversal alert points to an abbreviated output URL containing an ellipsis, not a filesystem traversal or path join operation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is an example hosted result URL in CLI output. No local path traversal logic is present in the reviewed skill file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Tool Allows RunComfy CLI Invocation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill grants Bash access for runcomfy commands. This is required for the skill, but it can submit remote jobs and write outputs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and command syntax are explicit. The scope is narrower than unrestricted Bash, but still performs external actions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network POST, Poll, and Download Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented workflow sends JSON to model-api.runcomfy.net, polls request status, and downloads output URLs from RunComfy domains.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: These lines describe the outbound endpoints and download allowlist directly. The pattern is expected but should be visible to users before publishing.

content.user_title: Run RunComfy Models from the CLI
content.value_statement: Teams need a repeatable way to call RunComfy models without manual browser steps. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through CLI authentication, job submission, polling, and output handling.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to install and verify the RunComfy CLI.
Guides browser and token-based authentication workflows.
Shows how to run a model with inline JSON input or input files.
Covers synchronous polling, no-wait submission, and status checks.
Documents JSON output mode for scripts and automation.
Describes output downloads, output directories, and retryable exit codes.
limitations: Requires the RunComfy CLI to be installed before model execution.
Requires a valid RunComfy account and token for authenticated requests.
Does not validate every model schema inside the skill file.
Generated media quality, timing, and cost depend on the selected RunComfy model.

use_cases.title: Automate media generation jobs
use_cases.description: Run image, video, edit, and lip-sync models from repeatable terminal commands.

use_cases.title: Build scripted model pipelines
use_cases.description: Use JSON output, no-wait jobs, and status checks to integrate RunComfy into scripts.

use_cases.title: Operate model runs in CI
use_cases.description: Use token-based authentication and explicit output directories in container or CI workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Check CLI readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Check whether the RunComfy CLI is installed, then tell me the next authentication step.

prompt_templates.title: Run one image model
prompt_templates.prompt: Use RunComfy to run a text-to-image model with this prompt and save the result in ./out.

prompt_templates.title: Submit and poll later
prompt_templates.prompt: Submit this RunComfy video job without waiting, capture the request id, and show me how to poll it later.

prompt_templates.title: Create a batch workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a RunComfy CLI batch workflow that reads prompts from a file, writes each output to a separate folder, and retries transient failures.

output_examples.input: Ask to verify installation and authentication.
output_examples.output: A short readiness report with the detected CLI version, current account status, and required next step.

output_examples.input: Ask to run a single model with a prompt and output folder.
output_examples.output: A terminal-ready command plan, expected job status flow, and the local path where results should appear.

output_examples.input: Ask for a batch generation workflow.
output_examples.output: A concise automation outline covering input files, per-job output folders, JSON parsing, and retry behavior.

best_practices: Use verified package managers for installation and inspect any remote installer before execution.
Keep RunComfy tokens out of prompts, logs, repositories, and shared terminal history.
Use explicit output directories so generated files do not mix with project files.
anti_patterns: Do not paste account tokens into chat messages or checked-in scripts.
Do not enable web search or remote reference URLs unless the user requested them for the task.
Do not copy shell examples with unreviewed user input into production automation.

faq.question: Does this skill install RunComfy automatically?
faq.answer: No. It explains installation options and then uses the CLI after the operator installs it.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it send data to RunComfy?
faq.answer: Yes. Model inputs are sent to RunComfy endpoints when a runcomfy command submits a job.

faq.question: Where are generated files saved?
faq.answer: By default files download to the current directory. The skill recommends setting an explicit output directory.

faq.question: How should CI authentication work?
faq.answer: Use RUNCOMFY_TOKEN in the environment and avoid logging the token or writing it into prompts.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is suitable to publish with a warning because it performs authenticated network actions and local file downloads.
